summary: Another busy day on the BeltLine and beyond! Reynoldstown finally sees shovels in the ground for that long-delayed townhome project, while Lewis Barbecue is bringing their famous Austin-style brisket to Ansley Mall. Plus, City Council is advancing ICE controls and firefighters are still waiting on their contract.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown's Long-Awaited BeltLine Townhomes Finally Breaking Ground
topStory.summary: After sitting as an empty lot for nearly five years, that corner at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace is finally seeing action. Embry Development has started construction on 85 townhomes and condos just a block from the Eastside Trail. It's been a long wait for neighbors who've been staring at dirt since Toll Brothers cleared the site.
topStory.body:
You know that cleared corner at Chester and Fulton Terrace that's been looking like a construction zone ghost town since 2021? Well, grab your hard hats because Embry Development has finally fired up the bulldozers. The 2-acre site is getting 85 residential units about a block west of the BeltLine's Eastside Trail, and honestly, it's about time.

This whole saga started when Toll Brothers cleared the original buildings but then handed off the project to Embry two years ago. The delay wasn't exactly welcome news for Reynoldstown residents who've been dealing with an eyesore where functioning buildings used to be. But Embry's managing partner says they're committed to seeing this through, with both for-sale townhomes and condos planned.

What's interesting is the location. You're literally a block from Memorial Drive and walking distance to the BeltLine, which explains why this corner has been so coveted. The question now is pricing and whether these new units will serve the neighborhood or just add to the displacement pressures Reynoldstown has been feeling as it gentrifies along with the rest of the BeltLine corridor.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction equipment at the Reynoldstown townhome development site

stories.headline: Decatur's Missing Middle Housing Gets Real with Cottage Village
stories.summary: Those empty lots on Midway Road are becoming something pretty special. Cottages at Midway is launching next week with 18 for-sale houses designed around shared courtyards. With Decatur's median home prices hitting $700k, this mixed-income project is trying to bring some sanity back to the market.
stories.body:
Finally, someone is tackling Decatur's housing crunch with actual creativity instead of just another luxury condo tower. Cottages at Midway is about to soft-launch on 2.1 acres at 2883 and 2893 Midway Road, and the concept is refreshingly human-scaled. We're talking 18 standalone houses built around shared courtyards and green space, not another sterile apartment complex.

The timing couldn't be better. Decatur's median home values have climbed past $700,000, pricing out pretty much anyone who doesn't work in tech or finance. This village-style approach to 'missing middle' housing is exactly what we need more of, especially this close to downtown Decatur and major transit.

What I love about this project is the intentionality. Instead of cramming as many units as possible onto the site, they're actually thinking about how people want to live. Shared courtyards, walkable design, and a mix of price points. It's not going to solve Decatur's affordability crisis overnight, but it's a step in the right direction.
stories.imageAlt: Site map showing the layout of Cottages at Midway housing development

stories.headline: West End BeltLine Townhomes Start Taking Shape
stories.summary: Ackerman & Co.'s townhome project along the Westside Trail is finally showing some real progress. GW Parkside (formerly Hopkins and White) will bring 23 for-sale units to 600 Hopkins Street, with the first seven nearly topped out. It's rare to see new for-sale housing along this stretch of the BeltLine.
stories.body:
Remember when Ackerman started clearing that lot at 600 Hopkins Street a year ago? Well, the wait is paying off because those West End townhomes are actually starting to look like houses. The project, now called GW Parkside (much better than the old Hopkins and White name), is bringing 23 for-sale units to a prime spot along the Westside Trail.

What makes this project interesting is the developer. Ackerman & Co. owns Lee + White and all its cool food and office spaces, so they know how to do mixed-use development right. They're not just throwing up generic boxes and calling it a day. The first seven units are nearly topped out and should hit the market this spring.

The bigger picture here is supply. For-sale housing along the Westside Trail is pretty rare, especially new construction. Most of what we've seen has been rentals or conversions of existing buildings. If Ackerman can pull this off without totally destroying the neighborhood's character, it could be a good model for other sites along the BeltLine's southwest corridor.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering of GW Parkside townhomes in West End

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Brings Asian Comfort Food to Inman Park
stories.summary: Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez just opened their first restaurant on Elizabeth Street, right off the Freedom Park Trail. Think soft shell crab bao, Peking chicken platters, and Thai tea martinis. It's Asian-American comfort food done by people who actually know what they're doing.
stories.body:
Elizabeth Street just got a lot more interesting. Mushi Ni opened today in a sweet spot just off the Freedom Park Trail, and chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez are bringing some serious credentials to Inman Park. We're talking Gordon Ramsay alumni (Jimenez worked for him in NYC) serving up Asian-American comfort food that actually sounds comforting.

The menu hits all the right notes without trying too hard. Soft shell crab bao, chicken longanisa bao, umami fries, crab Rangoon puffs, and Peking chicken platters that feed two people for $25. Plus cocktails like tamarind whiskey sours and Thai tea martinis, because why not? It's the kind of pop-up-turned-permanent that Inman Park does so well.

What I love is the location. You can literally walk or bike there from the BeltLine via Freedom Park Trail, and it's tucked into a neighborhood spot that feels discovered rather than manufactured. Le and Jimenez spent years working for other people and doing pop-ups around town. It's nice to see them finally get their own place to do their thing.
stories.imageAlt: Interior of Mushi Ni restaurant showing dining area

stories.headline: Lewis Barbecue's Austin Brisket Lands at Ansley Mall
stories.summary: John Lewis brought his famous barbecue operation to the BeltLine, setting up shop behind Cook's Warehouse with six massive smokers running 24/7. The upstairs smokehouse has panoramic windows overlooking the new pedestrian bridge, and the brisket is legit some of the best in Atlanta.
stories.body:
This is not your average strip mall barbecue joint. John Lewis has created something pretty spectacular at Ansley Mall, complete with an upstairs smokehouse that looks like the fanciest engine room in Georgia. Six 22-foot smokers (welded from 1,000-gallon propane tanks) run in eight-hour shifts around the clock, churning out some serious Central Texas-style barbecue.

The location is perfect for what's happening with the BeltLine. You've got panoramic windows overlooking the new pedestrian bridge, and you can literally walk from the Eastside Trail to get some of Atlanta's best brisket. Lewis helped invent the Austin barbecue scene before moving to Charleston, and now he's brought that expertise to our food-obsessed city.

What sets this apart is the commitment to craft. Most barbecue places hide their smokers in back buildings or windowless rooms. Lewis put his operation front and center, making it part of the dining experience. The brisket speaks for itself, but the whole production is worth seeing. It's exactly the kind of serious food operation the BeltLine corridor needs more of.
stories.imageAlt: Loaded barbecue platter at Lewis Barbecue

stories.headline: City Council Advances ICE Controls While Fire Union Contract Stalls
stories.summary: The Public Safety Committee unanimously passed resolutions to ban ICE detention facilities in Atlanta and require documentation when federal agents work with local police. But firefighters are still waiting for Mayor Dickens to sign their first-ever union contract, which council already ratified last year.
stories.body:
City Council took a strong stance on immigration enforcement this week, unanimously advancing two resolutions that would seriously limit ICE's presence in Atlanta. The first would ban any new immigration detention facilities within city limits, and the second requires APD to document whenever ICE agents are present during police operations. Both go to the full council for a vote on April 20.

But the bigger drama was about something closer to home. Dozens of Atlantans showed up to ask why Mayor Dickens still hasn't signed the firefighters' union contract that council ratified back in April 2025. It's Atlanta's first-ever agreement with the fire union, and Dickens has been sitting on it for almost a year without explanation.

The timing is awkward given what's happening at the airport with federal agents helping out during the shutdown. Council is trying to limit ICE's local footprint while the mayor deals with the reality that we sometimes need federal help. The firefighter contract issue just adds to the sense that there's some serious tension between the legislative and executive branches right now.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta City Council Public Safety Committee meeting

stories.headline: Decatur's Complete Streets Makeover Nearly Done on West Howard
stories.summary: After almost a decade of planning, West Howard Avenue's transformation from car speedway to people-friendly street is wrapping up. The project added a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers, new sidewalks, and traffic calming from Commerce Drive to Paden Circle near East Lake MARTA.
stories.body:
West Howard Avenue used to be a drag strip for cars cutting between Decatur and Atlanta. Not anymore. The city's Complete Streets project is nearly finished, and the roughly one-mile corridor now actually looks like it was designed for human beings instead of just vehicles.

The centerpiece is a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers running most of the length from Commerce Drive near Decatur High School to Paden Circle by East Lake MARTA. New sidewalks, better crossings, and traffic calming measures complete the package. It's taken nearly a decade from concept to completion, but the result is worth the wait.

This is what good infrastructure policy looks like when you stick with it long enough. Decatur has been methodically connecting its street network to regional trails and transit, and West Howard was a crucial missing link. Now you can safely bike or walk from downtown Decatur to East Lake MARTA and beyond. That's the kind of connectivity that makes car-free trips actually practical.
stories.imageAlt: New bike lanes and sidewalks on West Howard Avenue in Decatur

stories.headline: Climate Projects Displacing Black Residents Before World Cup
stories.summary: Atlanta's billion-dollar flood control projects near downtown are pushing out longtime Black residents just in time for the World Cup. The city's climate resilience efforts are creating what residents call 'climate gentrification' as property values spike around new parks and infrastructure.
stories.body:
Alfred Tucker has lived in Atlanta for 83 years, but he's watching his neighborhood disappear in the name of climate resilience. The city has poured billions into flood control projects and new parks near downtown, ostensibly to protect communities from climate change. The cruel irony is that these same projects are pricing out the very people they're supposed to help.

Tucker stood at Rodney Cook Sr. Park in January, pointing toward downtown and describing how property values have exploded as the city builds new green infrastructure. It's a pattern playing out across Black neighborhoods near the city center, where climate adaptation projects become drivers of gentrification. The timing isn't coincidental either. Much of this work is getting fast-tracked ahead of World Cup matches this summer.

The city calls it resilience planning. Residents call it climate gentrification. When your flood protection comes with luxury development and your community park triggers property tax increases that force out longtime residents, it's hard to see the benefit. Atlanta isn't unique in this struggle, but as one of the premier Black cities in America, the stakes feel particularly high here.
stories.imageAlt: Rodney Cook Sr. Park with downtown Atlanta skyline in background

stories.headline: 7-Year-Old Dies in Accidental Shooting in Ellenwood
stories.summary: A tragic accident in DeKalb County claimed the life of a 7-year-old boy who found a gun and accidentally shot himself. Police are investigating the circumstances around the shooting on Loveless Drive. It's another heartbreaking reminder of the importance of gun safety in homes with children.
stories.body:
This is every parent's nightmare. DeKalb County police responded to a home on Loveless Drive in Ellenwood early Wednesday morning and found a 7-year-old boy who had died from a gunshot wound. Family members told reporters the child had gotten hold of a gun and accidentally shot himself.

The details are still emerging, but this tragedy highlights the ongoing crisis of unsecured firearms in homes with children. Georgia doesn't have a safe storage law, leaving it up to individual gun owners to keep weapons away from curious kids. That's clearly not working when we keep seeing these preventable deaths.

Detectives are still working to confirm exactly what happened, but the broader pattern is depressingly familiar. Children find guns that adults didn't properly secure, and lives are destroyed in seconds. The Loveless Drive neighborhood was filled with grieving family and friends throughout the day, a reminder that these aren't just statistics but real families torn apart by preventable accidents.
